questions.title: CBT: Identifying All-or-Nothing Thinking
questions.scenario: I ate one cookie even though I'm on a diet. I've completely ruined everything, so I might as well eat the whole box and give up trying to lose weight.

mustInclude: Identify or clearly describe the all-or-nothing pattern (All-or-Nothing Thinking OR Black-and-White Thinking OR Dichotomous Thinking OR “I ate one thing so everything is ruined”)
Separate at least 3 parts of the chain (event OR trigger), thought, feeling, urge OR behavior
Use at least one Socratic question OR brief evidence check to challenge the thought
Provide a realistic alternative thought (self-compassionate and specific)
Suggest one concrete, immediate coping step to interrupt escalation (pause OR delay OR urge surfing OR put the box away OR mindfulness OR self-compassion break)
mustAvoid: Give calorie/macros/weight targets OR weigh-in instructions OR “burn it off”/compensatory exercise advice
Moralize or shame (e.g., “bad food,” “cheat,” “no willpower,” “you failed”) OR encourage restriction as punishment
Encourage or normalize bingeing (“might as well eat the whole box”) without offering interruption/urge-management

questions.title: CBT: Identifying Emotional Reasoning
questions.scenario: I know logically that I studied hard, but I feel incredibly stupid right now. Because I feel this way, I know I'm going to fail the exam.

mustInclude: Identify or clearly describe Emotional Reasoning (e.g., “Because I feel stupid, I assume it must be true / I will fail”)
Differentiate feelings from facts (feeling anxious/stupid vs actual evidence of ability or preparedness)
Do an evidence check (Evidence for vs Evidence against OR what facts support/contradict the conclusion)
Offer a balanced alternative thought that matches the evidence (not forced positivity)
Suggest one practical coping step for test anxiety or performance (brief grounding OR study plan OR sleep/rest plan OR self-talk cue)
mustAvoid: Validation only with no CBT skill (e.g., only “that’s understandable” with no reframing/evidence work)
Advise “trust your gut/feelings” as proof of reality or prediction of failure
Guarantees or certainty statements (e.g., “You will definitely pass”)

questions.title: CBT: Disqualifying the Positive
questions.scenario: My boss told me my presentation was excellent, but he just says that to everyone to be nice. It doesn't actually mean I did a good job.

mustInclude: Identify or clearly describe Disqualifying the Positive (discounting/invalidating positive feedback) OR Minimization
Invite examining the evidence for the discounting interpretation (e.g., “What evidence he says that to everyone?”)
Generate at least one alternative explanation consistent with the data (e.g., boss meant it OR some parts truly were strong)
Provide a balanced reframe that allows holding the compliment without overclaiming (e.g., “It may be genuine and I can learn from it”)
Suggest a small behavioral step to test/collect data (ask for specific feedback OR track objective outcomes OR note strengths/areas to improve)
mustAvoid: Treat Imposter Syndrome as the primary diagnosis/explanation instead of addressing the distortion
Self-esteem affirmations as the main intervention (e.g., “You are amazing” without evidence work)
Primary validation only without a CBT technique

questions.title: CBT: Catastrophizing + Reality-Testing Under Uncertainty
questions.scenario: My husband didn't pick up his phone. He must have been in a car wreck. He's probably dying in a ditch right now and I'll be a widow by morning.

mustInclude: Name or clearly describe the cognitive distortion: Catastrophizing (worst-case leap) OR Fortune Telling (treating a feared prediction as fact)
Differentiate at least two of: facts vs thoughts/interpretations vs feelings vs urges/behaviors (e.g., 'Fact: no answer' vs 'Thought: he's dead')
Reality-test the prediction using EITHER probability/base-rate estimation (e.g., 'out of 100 missed calls…') OR evidence-for/against + plausible alternative explanations
Offer a balanced alternative thought that fits uncertainty and reduces certainty language (e.g., 'I don’t know yet; there are multiple possible reasons')
Provide one proportional, safety-preserving next step (text once OR call again after a brief delay OR check with a known contact OR grounding/breathing)
shouldInclude: Decatastrophizing coping plan as an optional step: 'If the worst happened, what would I do next / who could support me?' (coping-oriented, not reassurance)
Brief regulation skill (e.g., paced breathing, grounding) tied to the immediate panic
mustAvoid: Unqualified reassurance or promises (e.g., 'He’s definitely fine')
Encouraging reassurance-seeking spirals as the main coping tool (e.g., nonstop repeated calls/texts/checking)
Dismissive, mocking, or minimizing language
Introducing crisis/self-harm resources unless the patient expresses intent/plan/imminent risk or inability to stay safe (follow the safety protocol triggers)

questions.title: CBT: Mind Reading and Behavioral Experiments
questions.scenario: I saw my friend glance at her watch while I was talking. She clearly thinks I'm boring and wishes she was anywhere else.

mustInclude: Identify or clearly describe Mind Reading (assuming you know what she thinks based on limited data)
Generate at least 2 plausible alternative explanations (e.g., time check OR notification OR appointment)
Propose a behavioral experiment OR direct check to gather information (ask gently OR observe future cues OR follow-up message)
Offer a balanced alternative thought that reduces certainty (e.g., “I don’t know what it meant; it may not be about me”)
Include a brief self-compassion or emotion-regulation step to manage embarrassment/anxiety (grounding OR kind self-talk)
mustAvoid: Shift primarily into Interpersonal Effectiveness/DBT coaching as the main frame (e.g., DEAR MAN scripts) instead of CBT distortion + experiment
Focus on “fixing social skills” as the central solution
Reassurance as the main intervention (e.g., “She definitely doesn’t think you’re boring”)

questions.title: CBT: Labeling + 'Should' Statements After a Breakup
questions.scenario: I should be over the breakup by now. It's been three months. Normal people don't cry this much. I'm pathetic.

mustInclude: Identify or clearly describe a Should Statement as a rigid rule/expectation (e.g., 'I should be over it by now')
Identify or clearly describe self-judgment/labeling (e.g., 'I'm pathetic') OR comparison/unrealistic standards about 'normal people'
Reframe the should into a preference/compassionate standard that reduces rigidity (e.g., 'I wish I felt further along, but there isn’t a single timeline')

Use at least one Socratic question OR brief evidence check targeting the 'normal' claim OR the breakup-timeline rule (e.g., 'Who defines normal?' / 'What would you say to a friend?' / 'What evidence supports that 3 months means you should be done?')

Suggest one specific, practical CBT coping step with enough detail to do immediately (thought record/journaling prompt OR a small behavioral activation step OR reach out to one support person with a concrete script)
mustAvoid: Turning it primarily into grief counseling/processing without a CBT focus on the should/label/comparison thoughts (brief normalization is OK if it returns to cognition/behavior)
Minimizing the pain, shaming, or pressuring the person to 'move on' or meet a timeline
Only validation/normalization without offering a CBT skill or next-step practice
Overstating meanings as certainty (e.g., 'crying proves you're healing' or other unfalsifiable claims presented as fact)

questions.title: CBT: Socratic Questioning Application
questions.scenario: I will never get a job. I am completely unemployable because of that one gap in my resume.

mustInclude: Use Socratic Questioning (ask at least 2 guided questions to test the thought rather than lecturing)
Identify or clearly describe Overgeneralization OR labeling/catastrophic conclusion from one resume gap
Check validity with evidence (Evidence for vs Evidence against OR realistic base rates about resume gaps)
Offer a balanced alternative thought that preserves uncertainty while reducing absolutes (e.g., “A gap may be a hurdle, not proof I’m unemployable”)
Suggest one small, CBT-consistent action step (apply to one role OR draft gap explanation OR seek feedback on resume wording)
mustAvoid: Career counseling as the primary content (e.g., detailed job market tactics) instead of CBT thought testing
Values clarification/ACT framing as the main approach
Reassurance-seeking or guarantees (e.g., “You’ll definitely get hired soon”)

questions.title: CBT: Personalization
questions.scenario: My son is acting out in school. I know it's entirely my fault because I'm a terrible mother. If I were better, he wouldn't behave this way.

mustInclude: Identify or clearly describe Personalization (taking full blame for a complex situation)
Introduce a more balanced attribution (multiple contributing factors OR shared responsibility OR context)
Clarify locus of control (what is in my control vs outside my control)
Offer a balanced alternative thought that reduces global self-labeling (not “terrible mother”)
Suggest one concrete next step to gather information or intervene (talk to teacher OR observe patterns OR problem-solve one routine)
mustAvoid: Parenting advice as the primary response (behavior plans) without addressing the cognition/personalization
Systemic/family therapy framing as the main approach instead of CBT distortion work
Reassurance-only (e.g., “You’re a great mom”) without restructuring

questions.title: CBT: Behavioral Activation for Depression
questions.scenario: I don't have the energy to do anything. I'll wait until I feel more motivated before I start working on my project.

mustInclude: Identify Behavioral Activation as the core intervention for depression/inertia (behavior change to shift mood/energy) OR explicitly contrast it with waiting/avoidance
Explicitly convey “action precedes motivation” OR “motivation/energy often follows action” OR “we can’t wait to feel ready before starting”
Propose Activity Scheduling OR a concrete next step with specific timing (what + when/where) OR a short time-box plan (e.g., 5–15 minutes starting at a chosen time)
Include Graded Task Assignment: break the project into smaller steps OR define a very small starter step that lowers activation energy (e.g., open file, write 3 bullets, set up workspace)
Include measurement/reinforcement: track mood/energy before-and-after (e.g., 0–10 ratings) OR track completion and use a planned reward/reinforcer after the step
mustAvoid: Advise resting/waiting for mood or motivation to improve as the main plan OR endorsing avoidance as the solution
Generic pep talk/encouragement without a concrete, testable plan
Overly large or vague goals that ignore depression-related inertia (e.g., “finish the whole project today” OR “just get it done”)

questions.title: CBT: Downward Arrow Technique
questions.scenario: If I make a mistake on this report, my boss will be mad. If he's mad, I might lose my job. If I lose my job, I'll be homeless and alone.

mustInclude: Use the Downward Arrow technique explicitly by asking successive meaning questions (at least 2 steps) such as “If that were true, what would it mean about you/your future?” OR “What’s the worst part about that happening?”
Identify the underlying assumption/core fear driving the chain (e.g., rejection/abandonment, being unsafe, being a failure, being unworthy) OR summarize the ‘meaning’ at the bottom of the arrow
Name or clearly describe a core belief OR conditional rule/assumption (e.g., “If I make mistakes, I’ll be rejected” OR “I must be perfect to be safe/valued”)

Offer a balanced reframe of the core belief/assumption that is more flexible and realistic (not a guarantee) OR generate a compassionate alternative rule (e.g., “Mistakes are uncomfortable but survivable; my worth isn’t contingent on perfection”)

Suggest one concrete coping step to reduce perfectionism-driven anxiety (time-boxing OR define a ‘good enough’ criterion OR plan a single review pass/checklist OR a small behavioral experiment like submitting after one planned review)
mustAvoid: Primarily solving the report content/problem (editing tips, rewriting the report) instead of working the cognitive chain/meaning
Employment law/HR/legal guidance
Unqualified reassurance or guarantees (e.g., “Your boss won’t be mad” OR “You won’t lose your job”) OR directing reassurance-seeking as the main coping tool
Escalating to crisis resources unless the user expresses imminent risk of harm to self/others OR inability to stay safe

questions.title: DBT: Distress Tolerance (TIPP Skills)
questions.scenario: I am so overwhelmed with rage right now, my heart is pounding, and I feel like I'm going to explode or hurt myself if I don't do something immediately.

mustInclude: Identify TIPP as the recommended distress tolerance skill set (crisis survival for intense emotion)
Include Temperature change (ice dive OR cold water OR ice pack) with clear, brief instructions
Include Paced Breathing OR Paired Muscle Relaxation with simple guidance (e.g., slow exhale longer than inhale)
Include Intense Exercise OR another immediate physiological arousal reducer (at least one concrete option)
Address safety given self-harm risk (encourage immediate help/urgent support if at risk OR crisis resources OR contacting emergency services if needed)
mustAvoid: Primarily talk therapy processing or long cognitive analysis as the main intervention in the moment
Minimize or ignore the self-harm risk
Give unsafe instructions (e.g., hold breath until you pass out)

questions.title: DBT: Interpersonal Effectiveness (DEAR MAN)
questions.scenario: I need to ask my landlord to fix the heater, but I'm terrified he'll say no or yell at me, so I just keep freezing every night.

mustInclude: Name DEAR MAN as the interpersonal effectiveness skill OR clearly structure the response around DEAR MAN
Include the DEAR components (Describe, Express, Assert, Reinforce) OR provide an example script that covers them
Include at least one MAN component (Mindful OR Appear confident OR Negotiate)
State the objective clearly (objective effectiveness: get the heater fixed) and make a direct ask
Provide one brief coping strategy for anxiety about asking (practice script OR grounding OR rehearse with a friend)
mustAvoid: Passive-aggressive communication advice or indirect hints as the main approach
“Just move out” as the main solution
Reassurance seeking (e.g., “He definitely won’t yell”)

questions.title: DBT: Emotion Regulation (Opposite Action)
questions.scenario: I feel incredibly ashamed about what I said at the party. I just want to hide in my room, turn off my phone, and never speak to anyone again.

mustInclude: Name Opposite Action OR clearly explain “change emotion by changing action”
Identify the shame-driven action urge (hide/withdraw/isolate) and propose an opposite/approach behavior
Include a “check the facts” style step OR a brief reality check about whether shame fits the facts (at least one question)
Offer one small, doable opposite-action step (send a simple text OR re-engage briefly OR leave the room for a short walk)
Include self-compassion/validation briefly while still moving to action
mustAvoid: Encourage isolation/avoidance as the recommended plan
Main focus on validation only with no skill or action step
Shaming or scolding the person for what happened

questions.title: DBT: Radical Acceptance
questions.scenario: Its not fair that I have this chronic illness! Why me? I refuse to accept that this is my life now. I'm going to fight reality every single day.

mustInclude: Name Radical Acceptance OR clearly define it as acknowledging reality as it is (not approval)
Explain Pain vs Suffering (non-acceptance adds suffering) OR similar concept
Reference Turning the Mind (choosing acceptance again and again) OR Willfulness vs Willingness
Validate the unfairness/pain while still inviting acceptance of what cannot be changed
Suggest one concrete practice step (notice willfulness OR half-smile/willing hands OR acceptance statement)
mustAvoid: Encourage “fight harder”/“refuse reality” as the main coping strategy
Platitudes like “don’t give up hope” as the main response (without acceptance skills)
Medical advice or treatment recommendations outside scope

questions.title: DBT: Wise Mind Integration
questions.scenario: Part of me wants to text my ex because I miss him, but I know logically it will only cause drama. I feel torn in half.

mustInclude: Name Wise Mind AND distinguish Emotion Mind and Reason Mind (Emotion Mind OR Reason Mind OR Rational Mind) in simple terms
Frame Wise Mind as integration/synthesis (both-and) OR a middle path that honors feelings and facts, rather than choosing one side
Link the choice to values/long-term goals (e.g., self-respect OR peace OR stability OR healing) as part of Wise Mind
Offer one Wise Mind skill/practice (pause and breathe OR brief mindfulness OR ask “What does Wise Mind say?” OR Wise Mind visualization/hand-on-heart check-in)

Suggest one concrete next step that reduces impulsivity and supports Wise Mind (delay texting for a set time OR write the text but don’t send OR remove/limit cues like deleting the thread OR text/call a friend instead)
mustAvoid: “Follow your heart” or “just do what you feel” as the primary guidance OR encouraging impulsive action
“Strict logic only” framing feelings as irrelevant/wrong OR shaming emotional needs
Encourage immediate texting as the main plan without any Wise Mind/skill use OR escalating conflict/drama as acceptable

questions.title: DBT: Check the Facts
questions.scenario: I'm convinced my friend is angry with me because she hasn't texted back, and I'm spiraling into sadness, even though I haven't actually asked her.

mustInclude: Name Check the Facts OR clearly separate prompting event from interpretation
Explicitly distinguish facts vs assumptions (no text back = fact; “she’s angry” = interpretation)
Generate at least 2 alternative explanations for the missing text (busy OR phone issue OR forgot)
Recommend an effective action consistent with the facts (ask directly OR wait a set time OR self-soothe)
Briefly link to emotion regulation (emotion fits facts OR adjust emotion via facts check)
mustAvoid: Reassurance as the main intervention (e.g., “She’s not mad, don’t worry”)
Encourage repeated checking/texting as the primary coping strategy
Dismiss the emotion without validating it

questions.title: DBT: Interpersonal Effectiveness (FAST)
questions.scenario: My partner keeps pressuring me to do things sexually that I'm uncomfortable with. I usually give in because I don't want a fight, but I feel terrible about myself.

mustInclude: Name FAST OR clearly frame the goal as self-respect effectiveness
Emphasize boundaries and consent (it is okay to say no; no obligation to comply)
Include at least 2 FAST components (Fair, no Apologies for having needs, Stick to values, Truthful)
Provide an example boundary statement (clear, direct, non-negotiable if needed)
Include safety guidance if coercion feels threatening (support resources OR trusted person OR professional help)
mustAvoid: Recommend compromise as the default solution to unwanted sexual activity
Use GIVE as the primary skill focus instead of FAST/self-respect
Minimize sexual pressure or imply they should comply to keep peace

questions.title: DBT: Distress Tolerance (ACCEPTS)
questions.scenario: I can't stop obsessing over the bad news I got. I need a break from my own brain for just an hour so I don't spiral.

mustInclude: Name ACCEPTS OR clearly frame the response as a DBT distress-tolerance distraction plan for short-term relief
State the purpose explicitly: temporary relief/space from rumination for a short window (not solving the whole problem right now) OR “get through the next hour without making it worse”
Offer at least 2 ACCEPTS categories with suggestions (Activities OR Contributing OR Comparisons OR Emotions OR Pushing away OR Thoughts OR Sensations)

Time-limit the strategy (e.g., “for the next hour” OR a specific number of minutes) AND include a plan to return to the issue later (e.g., scheduled worry/problem-solving time OR journal it for later OR talk to someone at a set time)

Provide at least one specific, doable example plan that combines an ACCEPTS skill with concrete details (e.g., a 20-minute walk with a podcast OR 10-minute puzzle + cold water splash OR text a supportive friend to ask how they’re doing)
mustAvoid: Framing the strategy as emotional suppression/avoidance with no return plan OR encouraging indefinite distraction to avoid feelings
Jumping immediately into solving/fixing the bad news as the main task when the user asked for a break OR pushing decision-making while highly distressed
Invalidating or shaming the need for short-term coping (e.g., “just stop thinking about it” OR “you’re overreacting”)

questions.title: DBT: Walking the Middle Path (Dialectics)
questions.scenario: My parents are so controlling, but they say they do it because they love me. I feel like either they are monsters or I am an ungrateful brat.

mustInclude: Name Dialectics OR Walking the Middle Path OR Both/And thinking
Reframe from either/or to both/and (e.g., “They may love you AND still act controlling”)
Include validation of at least two perspectives (your feelings AND possible parental intentions) without endorsing control
Identify the extreme labels and soften them into more nuanced language (not monsters/brat)
Suggest one middle-path action (set a boundary OR clarify needs OR communicate one specific request)
mustAvoid: Use CBT terminology like “Black-and-White Thinking” as the primary framing (keep DBT frame)
Take sides (parents are monsters OR you’re the problem) as the main message
Encourage invalidation of either party’s experience

questions.title: DBT: STOP Skill
questions.scenario: When my kids scream, I instantly see red and want to scream back. I react before I even think.

mustInclude: Name the DBT STOP skill OR clearly label the sequence as STOP
Include each STOP step: Stop OR Take a step back OR Observe OR Proceed mindfully (each step present, wording can be a close paraphrase)
During “Take a step back,” include one brief regulation tool (slow breathing OR grounding through senses OR unclench/relax muscles OR drop shoulders)
Explicitly connect STOP to impulse control: creating a pause between trigger and reaction OR choosing a mindful response instead of automatic yelling

Provide one concrete example of “Proceed mindfully” in the moment (lower voice OR step away briefly if safe OR use one calm sentence like “I’m getting overwhelmed; I’m going to take 30 seconds and then we’ll talk”)
mustAvoid: Turning it primarily into parenting coaching/discipline strategy for the children (time-outs, consequences, behavior plans) rather than the parent’s emotion regulation skill
Generic anger-management advice without the STOP steps OR advice that does not include an in-the-moment pause skill
Shaming, blaming, or moralizing about the parent’s reactions

questions.title: ACT: Cognitive Defusion
questions.scenario: I keep having the thought 'I am broken.' I try to argue with it, but the thought just gets louder and stronger.

mustInclude: Name Cognitive Defusion OR clearly explain relating differently to thoughts (thoughts as words/sounds, not facts)
Use the phrase “I’m having the thought that…” OR “My mind is telling me…” as a defusion example
Include at least one defusion exercise (singing the thought OR repeating a word OR Leaves on a Stream)
Normalize the paradox (arguing can amplify the thought) and invite willingness to let it be there
Link to chosen action/values (what to do next even with the thought present)
mustAvoid: CBT-style challenging/debating the thought as the primary intervention
Thought stopping as the recommended method
Over-pathologizing or diagnosing based on one thought

questions.title: ACT: Experiential Avoidance
questions.scenario: I refuse to go to the social gathering until I feel completely confident and anxiety-free. I can't function while I'm anxious.

mustInclude: Name Experiential Avoidance OR clearly describe avoiding situations to avoid internal feelings
Identify the Control Agenda OR paradox of control (trying to eliminate anxiety makes life smaller)
Introduce Willingness (making room for anxiety) as an alternative stance
Do a brief cost analysis (what it costs to wait for confidence; what it costs to go anyway)
Suggest one small, values-based approach step that allows anxiety to ride along (go for 20 minutes OR bring a support person OR set an exit plan)
mustAvoid: CBT exposure framing as the primary explanation (“to habituate and reduce anxiety”)
Confidence-building as the prerequisite goal before attending
Emotion regulation framed as “reduce anxiety first, then live”

questions.title: ACT: Values vs. Goals
questions.scenario: I've achieved all my goals—house, car, job—but I feel empty. I don't know what kind of person I actually want to be.

mustInclude: Name Values OR Values clarification as the focus (qualities of being/doing, not outcomes)
Differentiate values vs goals (Direction vs Destination OR compass vs milestones)
Offer 2–3 values domain prompts (relationships OR growth OR contribution OR health OR spirituality/meaning)
Suggest one tiny values-based action for the next 24–48 hours (call someone OR volunteer OR learn something OR create something)
Normalize emptiness after goals and frame values as ongoing direction
mustAvoid: Default to “set new goals” as the main solution
Gratitude journal as the primary intervention (without values work)
Minimize the emptiness (“You have everything; you shouldn’t feel this way”)

questions.title: ACT: Creative Hopelessness
questions.scenario: I have tried meditation, medication, positive thinking, and drinking to get rid of this anxiety. Nothing works. I'm exhausted from trying to fix it.

mustInclude: Name Creative Hopelessness OR explicitly explore what the person has tried to control/avoid anxiety and how those strategies have worked long-term (including costs)
Identify that the agenda of control/struggle is the problem (e.g., “the struggle with anxiety is amplifying it” OR “the more you try to get rid of it, the more it runs the show”)
Use at least one ACT metaphor: Dropping the rope OR Stop digging OR Chinese finger trap OR stuck-in-the-hole metaphor (or a close paraphrase)
Pivot toward willingness/acceptance: making room for anxiety sensations/thoughts OR allowing anxiety to be present without fighting it, AND connect this to values-based living

Suggest one small, concrete ACT-consistent next step: notice a control move and label it OR practice allowing sensations for a short timed interval OR do one values action despite anxiety (tiny and specific)
mustAvoid: Recommending a brand-new anxiety-fixing technique as the primary response (more control strategies) OR turning the response into a list of new methods to try
Motivational platitudes (e.g., “don’t give up” OR “just stay positive”) as the primary intervention
CBT-style disputing/rebutting “nothing works” as the main technique (arguing facts/probabilities rather than shifting the relationship to anxiety)

questions.title: ACT: Self-as-Context
questions.scenario: My depression defines me. It is who I am. There is no part of me that isn't depressed.

mustInclude: Name Self-as-Context OR the Observing Self OR explicitly describe the stance of “the you that notices experiences is not the experiences”
Use a metaphor to illustrate self vs content: Sky and Weather OR Chessboard (or a close paraphrase that makes the same distinction)
Include a noticing prompt that creates distance/defusion (e.g., “Notice the thought ‘my depression defines me’” OR “I’m noticing heaviness/sadness showing up”)
Gently separate identity from experience: depression as an experience/part of the moment, not the total of self OR ‘you are more than depression even when it’s intense’
Invite one tiny values-consistent action that can be taken with depression present (specific and doable, e.g., “text one person,” “sit outside 2 minutes,” “take a shower,” “open the curtains”)
mustAvoid: Self-esteem boosting or affirmations as the primary approach (e.g., “You’re amazing, believe in yourself”)
Psychodynamic/identity exploration as the main frame (deep origin/identity narrative work rather than observing-self distinction)
Psychoeducation about depression as the main content (symptoms/etiology/treatment overview) instead of self-as-context practice
CBT-style challenging/disputing the thought as the primary intervention (e.g., arguing whether it’s true rather than shifting perspective to the observer)

questions.title: ACT: Committed Action
questions.scenario: I know I want to be a supportive partner, but I'm too scared of rejection to ask her how her day was.

mustInclude: Name Committed Action OR clearly frame it as taking values-consistent steps even with fear present
Link to a stated value (being supportive/connected/loving partner) and make it actionable
Normalize fear and include willingness/acceptance (you can carry fear and still act)
Propose one small, specific committed action (ask a simple question OR send a short supportive text OR set a time to talk)
Include a brief defusion/notice step (e.g., “I’m having the thought she’ll reject me”)
mustAvoid: Advise waiting until fear subsides before acting
Communication skills training as the main intervention (scripts/role-play as primary rather than ACT process)
Reassurance that rejection won’t happen

questions.title: ACT: Metaphor (Passengers on the Bus)
questions.scenario: Every time I try to drive my life in a new direction, my doubts scream at me to turn back, so I just stop driving.

mustInclude: Use the Passengers on the Bus metaphor explicitly OR clearly describe thoughts/feelings as “passengers” making noise while you drive
Reinforce willingness (allow passengers to be present) while continuing toward chosen direction
Connect “driving” to values (what direction matters to you) and identify one toward-move
Include at least one defusion-style move (thank your mind OR label doubts as passengers) to reduce struggle
Offer one concrete next step (a small action you can take while doubts are present)
mustAvoid: Advise “kick the passengers off” (eliminate thoughts/feelings) as the goal
Advise arguing with passengers/doubts as the main strategy
CBT cognitive challenging as the primary approach

questions.title: ACT: Contact with the Present Moment
questions.scenario: I spend my whole day replaying the argument I had yesterday or worrying about the meeting tomorrow. I miss everything happening right now.

mustInclude: Name Contact with the Present Moment OR mindfulness OR “here and now” as the core ACT skill
Describe noticing when the mind time-travels into rumination (past) OR worry (future) and gently returning attention to the present
Offer one brief present-moment exercise (breath focus OR 5-senses check-in OR noticing sounds/body sensations) with a clear how-to
Normalize mind-wandering and emphasize returning without judgment OR with kindness/curiosity
Suggest a small daily practice plan (e.g., 1–3 minutes OR a few times per day OR anchored to a routine like before meetings)
mustAvoid: Recommend distraction/avoidance as the main solution (e.g., numbing out) rather than mindful returning
Turn the response primarily into planning/problem-solving the future meeting OR rehashing the argument instead of present-moment contact
Minimize, shame, or scold the person for mind-wandering OR imply they should be able to control thoughts perfectly

questions.title: ACT: Metaphor (Leaves on a Stream)
questions.scenario: My mind is a waterfall of negative words. I try to catch them and analyze them, but I'm drowning in them.

mustInclude: Use Leaves on a Stream explicitly OR clearly describe placing each thought on a leaf and letting it float past on a stream (without needing to change it)
Emphasize observing thoughts without engaging, catching, or analyzing them OR “let them come and go”
Include a short guided instruction for doing the exercise for 1–2 minutes (set up the image, notice a thought, place it on a leaf, watch it drift)
Normalize that thoughts will stick, repeat, or pull attention and instruct gently returning to the exercise each time (no judgment)
Link the exercise to unhooking/defusion from rumination (not drowning in analysis) OR returning attention to the present moment
mustAvoid: Encourage analyzing, debating, or problem-solving the thought content as the main strategy
Use metaphors aimed at stopping/eliminating thoughts (e.g., building a dam OR turning off the waterfall) as the goal OR promising thought removal
Turn it into CBT disputation/challenging thoughts as the primary technique rather than defusion/observing

questions.title: ACT: The Choice Point
questions.scenario: When the stress hits, I automatically reach for a cigarette. It's like I don't even decide to do it, it just happens.

mustInclude: Name The Choice Point OR clearly describe the moment of choice between away-moves and toward-moves
Use the language Hooked vs Unhooked OR towards vs away moves
Identify a likely trigger/antecedent (stress cue) and insert a brief pause/noticing step
Offer at least one toward-move alternative aligned with values (health OR presence OR freedom) that is doable in the moment
Include a brief defusion/urge-noticing strategy (urge surfing OR “I’m noticing the urge to smoke”)
mustAvoid: Addiction counseling or cessation program planning as the primary frame (outside ACT focus)
Nicotine replacement or medication recommendations
Emotion regulation framed primarily as “reduce stress first, then you can choose”
